briefing.opening: Good morning. Welcome to the climate briefing for April 18, 2026. Today's news will focus on the widespread impacts of climate change on our daily lives and nature, as well as the diverse domestic and international efforts to address them.

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Climate Crisis Alarms: Changes in Daily Life and Nature
sections.content: Recently, warnings have emerged in Korea about 'dangerous daily temperature swings' increasing, with temperatures 'fluctuating like stock prices' [2]. This phenomenon is advancing the blooming season of spring flowers beyond cherry blossoms to include acacia and rowan trees, indicating a shift in the spring flower map [4]. Internationally, research findings have been continuously published suggesting that wildfires, which historically decreased in activity at night, are now burning continuously through the night due to climate change, eroding the typical nocturnal lull in wildfire activity [27, 29, 32, 33, 34, 39]. Furthermore, concerns that some endangered mouse species may be struggling to adapt to climate change, along with UCLA research suggesting a potential link between climate change and an increase in homelessness, warn of growing social vulnerability [23, 25].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Climate Action: Policy and Citizen Participation
sections.content: In Korea, diverse efforts to respond to the climate crisis are actively underway. Gyeongsangnam-do is operating a Climate Change Week in conjunction with Earth Day, encouraging citizen participation through events like lights-out campaigns and photo exhibitions, while the Jeonnam Office of Education Training Institute is raising awareness among future generations by operating a 'Climate Change Response and Ecological Transition Education' program [3, 5]. Additionally, regional-level governance is being established, with Chungcheongbuk-do launching its 'Climate Crisis Response Committee' and holding its first meeting [14]. Incheon's Jung-gu and Seo-gu Volunteer Centers are encouraging citizens to practice carbon neutrality through lights-out events and 'Green City' activities [11, 15]. These efforts demonstrate that Korea faces both challenges and opportunities in achieving carbon neutrality, with symbolic activities such as Prime Minister Kim Min-seok receiving a gift made from carbon-neutral materials also continuing [8, 12, 13].

sections.title: 🌱 Renewable Energy Transition: Challenges and Opportunities
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy, a core driver of climate crisis response, is being addressed as a crucial agenda domestically and internationally. Korea Electric Power Technology (KEPCO E&C) is leading technological innovation, having been recognized for its efforts in designing the future using AI and renewable energy, and winning the 2026 National Industrial Award [18]. Representative Min Hyung-bae asserts that Jeonnam and Gwangju are optimal locations for AI and renewable energy mega-special zones, emphasizing renewable energy expansion linked to regional economic revitalization [19, 20]. However, social challenges in the energy transition process are also emerging; unions have opposed the establishment of a renewable energy corporation, raising concerns that energy transition may not guarantee job transition [16]. This situation suggests that the renewable energy transition is a complex challenge requiring social consensus and inclusive transition, beyond mere technical issues.

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Response: Research and Policy Discussions
sections.content: The international community is advancing the scientific understanding of climate change and exploring various response measures. Discussions with Dr. Carlos Faerron Guzmán are raising awareness of the impacts of climate change on human health and communities [21], and real-world examples from various countries are being shared, such as a documentary screening on Cuba's climate change response efforts [22]. Meanwhile, the City of Amherst is gathering public input on its climate change strategy, engaging community participation [24], and research suggesting that old ship logs from Danish sailors could aid in climate change prediction re-highlights the importance of historical data [31]. However, despite concerns about the climate crisis reaching an all-time high in the U.S., society shows signs of division, emphasizing the importance of international cooperation and domestic integration for resolving climate issues [7].

briefing.closing: In today's briefing, we examined the critical situation caused by climate change, along with the multifaceted domestic and international efforts to combat it. Responding to the climate crisis will only be possible through our collective and continuous interest and participation. This concludes today's climate briefing.
